speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't take us. The second time they took me to Leopoldsburg. The first time, they took us to a camp in Liege, see. And they didn't have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'll say so, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take you back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2 with the interview with Mr. Hyman Skulsky-- tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I made a little error--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll take you back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, good. Anyway, they took us from-- oh, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask the question.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the time you were taken to an internment camp around June of 1940. Will you pick up from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, actually, they took us to a city called Liege, which is quite a big city. In the center of the city, on the mountain-- the highest spot in the city-- there's an old fortification called the Citadel. And it's there they took us. And it's very old, built of stone. And they-- we-- we were taken there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were there with you at that Citadel?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, probably around 600-- maybe a little less, but quite a lot. And the Citadel-- from the Citadel, you could see the whole city of Liege. It's a very nice spot, high up. The only thing was that the moment we arrived there, the SS troopers who transported us left. And we were taken care of by the Wehrmacht. These are the regular soldiers. And they were much better than the SS. And we were given food. And we had no idea how long we were going to stay. That was nothing like the place in Brussels where we'd stayed-- the exhibition hall. But anyway, we settled down. And we spent a few days there. And on the Tuesday-- exactly a week after I had been arrested-- a German soldier comes in and calls my name and another name who was a friend of mine, Max Schlusney. We knew each other. He was also born in London about the same time as I was. And I knew-- his family knew our family. And this German soldier calls our two names, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We had take the luggage along. We should come with him. And we follow him. And he goes with his rifle, takes us out of the camp. And we're walking downhill to the city of Liege. And he takes us into the [? Kommandantur, ?] which was the German headquarters, which were in the city hall of Liege. And we come in there. And we go into an officer's room. And the man said, yes, [GERMAN]. We are free, Schlusney and I. And you know, we couldn't believe it. What the reason is, we didn't know. I had no idea. But anyway, if he says we are free, we are free. And we're coming out of the city hall to the street. And there's-- my brother's waiting, Sam and Schlusney's sister. Schlusney had a sister living in Liege. She was married. And she had a shop there. And both of them went to the [? Kommandantur ?] to the commanders there. They heard we were there. And they explained to them-- they said, we were only born in England. We never lived there for a long time. And we are Belgian-- the whole story. And they listened to them. And they said, OK, these two we're going to release. So both of us were released. And we were free, and thanks to Sam. He found out where we were. And he put a good word in. Anyway, we came home--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to the other people that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the citadel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We joined them. Two years later, we-- I joined them. Anyway, for a year and a half, I was at home. I was not allowed to leave home for the night. At night, I had to be in my home where I lived. We had lots of regulations, Jewish regulations. Jews and dogs were not allowed into the parks. Lots of things that-- we had to register as Jews. Our identity cards were stamped with a stamp on it saying, Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, in 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, I went to Brussels. My brother lived in Brussels, the one, Abraham, that's the older brother that passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to his house and rang the bell. And his landlady opened the door. I said, I'd like to speak to my brother. She says, they arrested him yesterday morning. I said, him? What for? She doesn't know. I said, where is he? No idea. He was arrested, and he disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the police. I went to the Germans. Nobody knew a thing what happened to him. So I went home, and I told my parents. And we had no idea for what reason or what happened to him. Anyway, another young Jewish man was arrested the same day. And his parents found out where he was, their son. So I presumed my brother must be in the same place. It was a concentration camp between Antwerp and Brussels called Breendonk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What reason? They didn't know. Anyway, we found out later that-- this was in July, I believe-- in July, the Belgians celebrate their independence day. I believe it's the 19th. And students held a-- what do we call it, manifestation or something? Demonstration.

speaker: interviewee
text: Demonstration against the Germans at the Belgium-- Belgium Unknown Soldiers Monument. And although my brother had nothing to do with it or the other man had nothing to do with it, they arrested-- I don't know how many, whether it's 20 or 50 people, just innocent people, and took them as hostages, put them in the concentration camp. Anyway, we had six months that he was there. It was a bad one. I mean, not as bad as in Germany. But they killed a lot of people there in these camps. And my brother belonged to a musical society. And the Queen Mother-- Queen Elizabeth of Belgium-- was the honorary president or something. So somebody who knew him wrote to her and explained, maybe she could do something for him. And apparently, through her interference, he was released six months later. So he came out and this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little about your life for this year and a half or while you were free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our group, [HEBREW], continued. But as we had to stay in the-- we were not supposed to-- I was not supposed to leave the town or even the home, we used to organize-- every meeting we had-- one a month at that time-- we used to go to a home-- all of us-- meet in the home, and stay the whole night, sleep on the floor. In the morning, 7 o'clock, we used to go home. Well-behaved-- everybody was well-behaved. And this went on till the-- till the week before I was arrested, till 1942, April the 29th '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this period, did you do any work? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did some business in the bicycle accessory business. I used to-- I had old customers. I used to go to them, see them, and call them up. And everything was black market, by the way. Nothing was kosher at that time anymore. Whatever we did, was black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Dad working at the time as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. He used to go in to business. But every day or every other day, somebody was arrested there for one reason or another. In the diamond bourse-- he used to go to the diamond bourse every where-- every day. And the Germans used to come in, arrest a man, take him along. Then, they had to pay a sum. This was a kind of blackmailing. They used to accuse him-- oh, he's done business without booking it. So for $5,000 or 5,000 francs-- I don't remember-- we can get you out of here. And they paid up, and they went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the availability of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was very hard. We used to have to stand in line, sometimes for hours. I remember, one time, there was a line. And I asked what it was for. They said it was for herring, for matjes herring. So I joined the line. I stood there for over two hours. I got-- I got one or two matjes herrings, I-- bread was the same thing. We used to have to stand in line. And it was quite a problem. Foods was a big problem at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the-- your life generally. Did people still go to temple? To shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Even I used to go to temple every morning. My father-- [HEBREW]-- said-- one day to me, he said-- you know, there are not too many people coming to shul. And we need a minyan. Come as well. I said, OK. So I went every morning to shul, every morning the last year before I was arrested. Not that I wanted to, but I did him a favor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the attendance at the minyans were meager?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cut down. Lots of people had left. And people were leaving every day, on the quiet. People didn't say, we're going away. They just disappeared. They left with smugglers. People used to-- you couldn't go by yourself. You had to have somebody to show you what way to-- you had to go to a village and stay there the night. And the next day, they would come-- somebody would come-- and take you across a field where there's no guards or things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what it was like, at that time, during the high holy days?

speaker: interviewee
text: High holy days-- it was like any other one, with the exception that people were more worried. And they were more serious. And they took praying very seriously at that time, because it got worse. The situation got worse from week to week. And we could see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, one day, they said, all the Jews have to register. Those who were clever did not. But we thought-- and you had to register not with the Germans. You had to register with the Jewish-- what did they call it? The Jewish-- like the federation, for instance, here. It was a trick the Germans played on the Jews. They thought, oh, why shouldn't you register with the federation? But they did. And then, the Germans went in there, had all the lists.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in 19-- I was arrested already, the second time-- the Jews had to wear the stars. And they sent one for me as well. But I was in prison already. So I didn't have to wear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're arrested the second time. And then, what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on a Wednesday night-- April 28, 1942-- a girl-- another English girl-- a Jewish girl-- comes to our house. I wasn't at home at that time. But my mother was home. My father was home. She said, we are friends with a policeman-- a certain policeman. And he gave us a hint today that tomorrow morning they're going to arrest all the British subjects that are still free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came to warn me if I wanted to escape or run away. I couldn't escape, because my parents were there. If I would run away, God knows what they would do to my parents. So when they told me-- they told me after dinner. They didn't want to spoil my dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went up to my room and I packed a few things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took some pictures. I said, in case if they come, I'll be ready for them. The next morning, I got up. And also, early, around 7 o'clock, there was a knock on the door. And I opened the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, Mr. Skulsky? I said, yes. They said, you've got to come along with me. So I knew what was going to happen, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to the diamond bourse, one of the diamond buildings, where-- they took us there. And a German guard was standing in front of a room. And it was filled with people already, with other prisoners. By the way, Americans as well-- at that time, we were together with Americans. And we spent the day there till about 4 o'clock. At 4 o'clock, a bus came along, a big bus. And it parked right against the wall. There was no space between the wall and the building. And the door of the bus went into the door of the building, of the diamond bourse. So you couldn't escape, especially then. They took us then. They loaded us up. And they took us to this camp, which I mentioned before, Breendonk, 40 or 50 miles away from there. And they put us in there. And we spent six months there in this particular camp, which was not too bad, because we could receive visitors [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- on Sundays only. Yeah, visitors came on Sundays only. And then, we could also go home for three days' leave if we had a good reason for it. I went home. I needed to see a dentist. I had my own dentist. So they said, OK. You have to sign three days' leave. And you have to be back in three days. Otherwise, you know what will happen. Anyway, I went twice-- three times, I went. In the six months, I went three times. And things were very bad in Antwerp at that time for the Jews. They had-- just had started to pick up the Jews, just round them up in the streets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, on one of my leaves, I went to see a friend of mine that lived just around the corner from us and with a grandmother. The grandmother was a woman who lived to do good.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in her 80s. And she would go to hospitals, help people, bring them food, bring them books, bring-- pray with them. Made-- you know what [HEBREW] is to a couple? Matchmaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matchmaking. And she would only do it if she knew of a person who needed some help. She would go around to the diamond businesspeople and ask them, I need some money for somebody. She wouldn't say who it was. And everybody gave, and she helped this person. Well, I came there. And the Germans were there. And they arrested her while I was there. They would have arrested me as well. But I had a certificate from my camp that I was a prisoner already. So they said, [GERMAN], get away. Otherwise, they would've taken me as well. And they took her and her son and the grandson, while I was standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the name of this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Morgenstein. I remember her very well. I can see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name? Do you remember what her first name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name? No. I knew the whole family. One grandson, lives in Israel. He's a big mack in the diamond business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I returned to the camp. And as I say, we had visitors. My mother came. My father was afraid to come, because he wore a beard. And he was afraid. And friends came. And then, one day, on a Saturday, they told us, on Monday morning, we're leaving. Just from one day to the next, no-- and they put us on a train. They sent us to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It took about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] five days, six days traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a train was it? Was it regular passengers?

speaker: interviewee
text: A regular passenger train. I mean, not first class. It was a third-class passenger train, yes. And we were fed on the way. And we arrived. And we joined the old prisoners which we had left two years earlier. They were there in the same camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners from the citadel?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Citadel. The whole group was there. And I had several friends there, as well, from Antwerp. And they were-- they were glad to see me. And I was glad to see them. You know, it was like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at least, we came there. We knew somebody. And the life there was not too bad. We were prisoners. The food was every week or every two weeks or every three weeks-- depended on transports-- we used to get a package from England, a Red Cross package, with 15 food items in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this camp was where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- it-- at that time, it was Germany. It's Upper Silesia, Oberschlesien. Now, it's Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kreuzburg. Kreuzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living accommodations like? Both in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 16 to a room, 8 double beds, one on top of the other-- bunks. And I had very nice people in my room. I had several Israelis-- I mean Palestinians. There was no Israel at that time. And they lived like-- like kibbutzniks. There were eight in our room. They used to share everything, all the food they had. They used to cook together and divide by 8. And one man used to take care of their food for the whole week, cleaning, cooking, I mean, whatever there was to do. There was not much to do, but still. And every week, they used to change. Another man took over. I didn't belong to this group, but I was very friendly with them. In fact, one man by the name of [INAUDIBLE], he said to me-- he said, if you'd like to, I'll teach you Hebrew. I said, with pleasure. I'd love to. So twice a week, he gave me Hebrew lessons, and-- which passed away the time much faster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, another friend of mine [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the name of David-- David-- doesn't matter-- he said, you know what? Let's learn Spanish in camp. I say, good. So I took Spanish classes. And I took Hebrew classes. And we got books for the Spanish. We got from the YMCA or the Red Cross. I don't remember. They sent us books to study. So life in both those camps was not bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing like a concentration camp. The only thing-- the worry that we had-- this was the worst of everything-- we worried about our families. We had no idea what happened. Till one day, I received a postcard from my father-- just a postcard-- which said, we are on our way to Germany. And we are OK. My father, my mother were together, he said. And I hope you are OK. And he had thrown the postcard out of the window of-- out of the train. And somebody posted it for This was the last I heard of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where they were heading for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no idea, because at that time, we had no idea about the death camps. That came much later. I mean, they were in existence. But nobody knew about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been able to find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They registered in Yad Vashem. And in Belgium, I also got a notification after the war that they had been taken to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both murdered there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, after Kreuzburg, you're there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kreuzburg-- we spend 10, January 1945. And then, the Russians made their big push forward, if you remember the-- the history. So one-- one day, they announced-- there was a board with notices as well, for the prisoners. They asked-- they wanted anybody who wants to sit for an exam, an exam that came from England, from one of the universities. What was it for? Nothing hard, like a school exam.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I'm doing nothing anyway. Why not? So I signed up. And they said, OK, tomorrow morning at 8 o'clock, you'll come to the mortuary. That's where they gave the exams-- and the whole day till lunch, and then after lunch, and then in the evening two hours. I said, OK, good. So I went there. And I sat through the exam till 12 o'clock. I come out at 12 o'clock from the first part. I see there's a whole commotion in the camp. I say, what's going on? We are leaving the camp tomorrow, yeah, next morning. I say, what do you mean you're leaving camp? We're leaving the camp. They're taking us away from here. One day notice they give you. Were there any rumors floating around about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew the news. We knew all the-- we had the-- we had a secret radio in the camp. And every evening, they gave us the news, the real news, not the German news. And so I said, I'm not doing anything. I'm not going to pack till I finish the exam. I started it. I'm going to finish it. So I did. And when I finished it, the papers were supposed to be sent back to England through the Red Cross. Anyway, when I finished, I started packing my belongings. And you wouldn't believe how much you accumulate, even in a prisoner of war camp. There's so much. Everybody-- I mean, we left most of it behind. But everybody was loaded. And by the way, this was the coldest winter of the war, '45. It was the worst. The snow-- the ice and snow was very-- 20 inches. And next morning, we had to schlep to the station and on the ice. And we had to wait for-- I don't know how many-- two or three hours-- before a train come-- came. And the train was a cattle train. This was really a cattle train. And they-- we went in there. And we were traveling till next morning. We went-- we left Kreuzburg at about 1 or 2 o'clock in the afternoon. And we arrived at 6:00 next morning in Breslau. It's a big town, which is also Poland now, I believe, Breslau. And there, they took us to a school. And I remember that in this train, I was sick. I had diarrhea. There was no room to-- to stand, yes, but I was sitting in the corner with my legs folded up. And I couldn't move, all the way to Breslau till next morning. And when we came to the school I was sick as a dog. But the next day, I felt a bit better. And we stayed there from Friday night-- Saturday morning till Monday morning. On Monday morning, they put us on another train. And we travelled for 10 days to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a cattle car or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was a passenger-- a regular passenger train, yeah. But we had an experience on this trip as well. We stopped in one of the stations-- I don't remember which one-- on the side line, not in the station, but just outside the station. And next to ours, a train stopped-- an open train, like a cattle train but open. And it had prisoners in there from a concentration camp. And they had dead bodies in them. And they were begging for food. They had nothing-- and frozen. So some of our prisoners threw things out. I mean, we would all give them anything. But the Germans came and stopped us, the guards. And-- but I'm afraid to think of the views we saw in this train. It was terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were like animals. I don't know whether they're Jewish or not. But they were just like animals. And they were in an-- terrible weather, the coldest weather you can imagine, in an open train. And they where traveling. Where they took them or where they came from, we have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why, Mr. Skulsky, do you think that you were on this passenger train? Was it had to do with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Russians were coming. So they-- they were going to occupy the town of Kreuzburg. So they took us down to Austria to a place called Spittal an der Drau. It's in southern Austria, on a straight line above Venice, but it was in Austria. When we came there, the snow was-- I mean, we jumped out of the train. There was at least two feet of snow and lovely scenery. It was all mountains all covered in ice and snow. And they took us to the last camp. We-- we spent three more months there. OK.
